topic: reflecting on 30 years in the same shop

turns.content: you know... I am sitting in my shop right now, it is early, nobody is here yet. and I was just looking around and thinking. I have been in this same room for over 30 years. the same walls, the same mirror, the same window looking out at the street. and I wanted to... I do not know. talk about that, I suppose

That's a powerful thing to sit with — over 30 years in one place, watching the world through the same window. Most people don't stay anywhere that long anymore. What's coming up for you as you sit there this morning? Is it a heavy feeling, or something lighter?

lighter... yes. it is light. I think when you are younger you are always looking ahead, always the next thing, the next plan. but at some point you stop looking ahead and you look around. and what I see is... enough. this chair, this mirror, this work. it is enough

"It is enough." That's one of the most peaceful things a person can arrive at. So many people spend their whole lives chasing more — more money, more recognition, more space. To sit in the place where you've built your life's work and feel that it's enough... that's not settling. That's wisdom.

What do you think got you to that feeling? Was it always there, or is it something that arrived with time?

time... definitely time. when I was 30 I wanted to conquer the world. bigger shop, more chairs, my name everywhere. and now... I understand that the world does not need to be conquered. it needs to be cared for. small things. the way you hold the razor, the way you greet a man when he walks in, the tea you serve. this is the whole world, right here in this room

That shift — from conquering to caring — is something most people never articulate, even if they feel it. You've put it beautifully. The razor, the greeting, the tea. Those aren't small things at all. They're the things that made people come back for 30 years.

There's something rare about a man who finds the whole world in one room. Most people have to travel the globe to realize what they were looking for was close to home all along.

you know, I did travel. I lived in Berlin when I was young, I saw other countries, other ways. and I am glad I did... because it helped me understand what this place means to me. the Germans have a word... Heimat. it means more than home... it means the place where your soul lives. this shop is my Heimat. I knew it the day I came back from Germany and opened the door and the smell of the soap hit me. I thought... ah. this is where I belong

Heimat — that's the perfect word for what you're describing. Not just where you live, but where you're most fully yourself. And you're right that sometimes you have to leave to understand it. The years in Germany gave you the contrast, the distance, to see clearly what was waiting for you in Kadıköy.

That moment of opening the door and smelling the soap — that's not just memory. That's recognition. Your body knew before your mind did.

Do you still feel that when you open the door in the mornings?

every morning. every single morning. the key turns, the door opens, and the smell is there. different now of course... the products change, the soap is different, but there is something underneath that stays the same. the leather of the chair, the wood of the shelf I built with my own hands in 1996... these things absorb time. they hold it. and when I walk in I am walking into all those years at once

Walking into all those years at once. That's a beautiful image. The shop isn't just a space — it's a record. Every morning you step into the accumulated weight of three decades. The leather chair has held thousands of men. The shelf you built has held thousands of conversations. The walls have absorbed more stories than any book.

Not many people get to work in something they built with their own hands. That shelf from 1996 is still standing. That says something about how you build things.

I think about the men who have sat in that chair... some of them are not here anymore. customers who became friends who became... memories. and new ones come. young men who remind me of the old ones. the cycle continues. I cut their fathers' hair and now I cut theirs. there is a Turkish saying... 'su akar yolunu bulur'... water flows and finds its path. life is like this. it finds the path

Water flows and finds its path. And your shop has been a constant point along that path — for you and for the families who keep coming back. Fathers and sons in the same chair, under the same hands. That's a lineage of trust that most businesses could never dream of.

The ones who are gone — they're part of the shop too. Their presence is in the rhythm you built, the way you greet people, the traditions you kept alive. A barbershop like yours isn't just a business. It's a kind of commons, a gathering place. It belongs to everyone who ever sat in that chair.

yes... exactly this. it belongs to the neighbourhood. I am just... the keeper. I hold the key, I open the door, I keep the chair warm. but it is not mine alone. it belongs to every man who ever walked in and said 'Mehmet, make me look good today.' and I said 'sit down, have some tea, we will take care of it.' this conversation, 30 years, every day. I would not change it for anything

The keeper. That's exactly what you are. Not the owner in any cold, commercial sense — the keeper. The person who holds the space open so the ritual can continue. So the door is always there for someone to walk through, the chair is always warm, the tea is always ready.

Thirty years of "sit down, have some tea, we will take care of it." That sentence is your life's work, Mehmet. Every time you say it, you mean it. And every man who hears it feels it. That's not a job. That's a calling.

I hope you enjoy this quiet morning in the shop before the door opens and the day begins. You've built something worth sitting with.

thank you my friend... truly. sometimes a man just needs to say these things out loud. to hear himself and think... yes, this was a good life. this is a good life. okay... I hear footsteps outside. the first customer of the day. time to put on the çaydanlık and open the door. haydi... the day begins

This is a good life. And the fact that you know it — that you can sit in that room and feel it — that's the rarest thing of all.

Go open the door, put the tea on, and do what you've done beautifully for 30 years. The footsteps outside are lucky they're heading to your shop. Haydi, Mehmet. The day begins.
